But the point is thereвҖҷs plenty of used packs. We know that itвҖҷs inevitable theyвҖҷre going to come back. So thatвҖҷs to the like, is it a boutique distraction? No, I think itвҖҷs a real durable business. And to the question, is it a distraction? We are still quite committed to the metals business. I think thatвҖҷs still so exciting and so important to bring back to North America.

And this does seem like, to me at least, this seems just to fit right in. Like in a sense, itвҖҷs almost like a version of recycling. YouвҖҷre sort of recycling power. YouвҖҷre recycling the power before you recycle the metals.

I was trying to think about how to say this.

But in the same way, youвҖҷre preventing new stuff from having to be made. I mean, in a sense, in both cases, youвҖҷre preventing the necessity for new stuff.

Yeah, I mean, the need for new stuff is gigantic. And so in order to fuel a complete electrification of the economy, thereвҖҷs still a lot of new stuff weвҖҷre going to need, but we can in the short term blunt that a little bit and make it cheaper by extracting really every last drop of utility, whether it be energy storage capacity, whether it be the metals out of old things. And then if you fast forward 100 years, like itвҖҷll all be circular.

So this is, especially in these depressing current times. These are among, among the places I go, my happy places is thinking about this 100-year time horizon and total circularity. And itвҖҷs interesting to think how this fits into that picture, basically. You can imagine a world of full circularity vis-Г -vis metals, right, where we no longer are having to dig up any new metals, basically. WeвҖҷve reached a steady state. WeвҖҷre perfectly recycling all the metals that come through.

You can. The thing I want to emphasize is that that also just makes economic sense. It is a cheaper way to provide nickel and lithium in addition to being sort of lower carbon and all of the other things that circularity provides.

Yes. Less grim labor implications, all that kind of stuff as well. But I was sort of thinking about how this fits into that. Like if youвҖҷre getting all the worldвҖҷs batteries and recycling them and youвҖҷre sucking all this capacity out of them before you recycle them, youвҖҷre also like, thatвҖҷs a big, potentially a big chunk of the energy storage we need. Like what, what sort of вҖ” like how much of the stationary storage market in the US do you think could ultimately be served by second life batteries?

5 gigawatt-hours a year. And this plant you built, we should say, this off-grid plant, itвҖҷs like 63, not, itвҖҷs not even 100 megawatt-hours. So to really scale this up to start matching the input, thatвҖҷs just going to be a lot of land, isnвҖҷt it? YouвҖҷre not worried at all about the land?

Some of them will be grid connected. Like if you can imagine a place where somebody has put a utility-scale battery, we have a project like that. We have grid-connected grid arbitrage. We have behind the meter, front of the meter. We have paired with manufacturing, paired with renewables.

Any energy storage plays, basically.

Exactly. Same energy storage as any other kind.

As I think about the logistics, new things keep coming to mind. But in terms of your battery gathering, how many sites do you have where youвҖҷre gathering batteries? Because wherever you build one of these, you are establishing basically an ongoing relationship to some source of these batteries, right? A supply chain. YouвҖҷre going to have to have batteries continually shipped between where theyвҖҷre gathered and where you have this plant. How many of those battery gathering sites are there?

As this becomes a national project for us, we need sort of regional warehousing.

Is it all in the one Nevada plant at this point?

ItвҖҷs not. We also have a big site that weвҖҷre developing and actually just opened our first recycling plant there in South Carolina. So thatвҖҷs a strategic spot for us on the east coast, near a port, near a lot of industry. And thatвҖҷs another logical place for us to inventory this stuff. But I imagine, in the fullness of time, we will put them where it makes sense to support these customers.

I just keep trying to wrap my head around the shift that you are about to undergo. Like right now you say you got 5 gigawatt-hours a year of batteries incoming.

Yep, gigawatt-hours, sorry. IвҖҷm assuming that the line is going up and to the right.

How fast per year? Like, whatвҖҷs next year? How steep is that line? I mean, IвҖҷm assuming itвҖҷs just going to get real steep relatively soon. But I donвҖҷt know if youвҖҷve actually charted it out and plotted it out.

Yeah, itвҖҷs a little hard to predict, but weвҖҷve seen 50% year-on-year growth at the beginning of this wave. And you can look back at the beginning of EV manufacturing in 2012 in volume. What was the ramp rate? And weвҖҷre going to see something like that.

Interesting. So weвҖҷre going to go from like 5, 10. Like having 100 GWh of batteries coming through is not crazy. Not out of the...

Yeah, I mean, you can almost just predict it. WeвҖҷre making 100 gigawatt-hours today, so in 10 years weвҖҷll be seeing those come back ready for second life.

And so then I just, at that level of volume, getting more power out of them will in no way be a small thing. I think this is what J. B. Straubel was kind of trying to get at in his big talk вҖ” this may look like a side thing today, but itвҖҷs going to be enormously important. If youвҖҷve got 100 gigawatt-hours coming through, thatвҖҷs a lot of power to just be throwing away. So itвҖҷs just going to be a lot more significant in five years.

It really will. So we are laying the groundwork for this. This team knows how to build things at scale. We have done it before. The business and the power electronics, the mechanical bits that weвҖҷre designing for energy storage, theyвҖҷre all designed for mass manufacture, for simple assembly in the field. Because we know that this is coming вҖ” tens and hundreds of gigawatts.

Well, I love to see someone getting ahead of the puck. As I look around these days at US politics, itвҖҷs nice to see someone anticipating whatвҖҷs going to happen and getting ready before it happens. Just a beautiful thing to witness.

Thank you. WeвҖҷre having fun. We love finding something valuable that hasnвҖҷt been extracted and going after it. And this is just another example of it.

Yeah, itвҖҷs just a bunch of puzzles. I mean, all this second life stuff is just an enormous logistical puzzle to solve. And solving puzzles is fun.

Colin, thanks so much for coming on and talking us through this. Really interesting. I think itвҖҷs a lot bigger of a deal than it sort of appears on the surface. So it was fun to вҖ” fun to dig in a little bit with you. Thanks for coming on.

So fun to be here. Great questions. Really enjoyed it.

Thank you for listening to Volts. It takes a village to make this podcast work. Shout out, especially, to my super producer, Kyle McDonald, who makes me and my guests sound smart every week. And it is all supported entirely by listeners like you. So, if you value conversations like this, please consider joining our community of paid subscribers at volts.wtf. Or, leaving a nice review, or telling a friend about Volts. Or all three. Thanks so much, and IвҖҷll see you next time.

items.title: Armed police swarm student after AI mistakes bag of Doritos for a weapon
items.author: antongribok
items.content:
Concerns over AI surveillance in schools are intensifying after armed officers swarmed a 16-year-old student outside Kenwood High School in Baltimore when an AI gun detection system falsely flagged a Doritos bag as a firearm.

Taki Allen was hanging out with friends after football practice on October 20 when multiple police cars suddenly pulled up.

вҖңIt was like eight cop cars that came pulling up for us,вҖқ Allen told WBAL-TV 11 News. вҖңThey started walking toward me with guns, talking about вҖҳGet on the ground,вҖҷ and I was like, вҖҳWhat?вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңThey made me get on my knees, put my hands behind my back, and cuff me. Then they searched me and found nothing,вҖқ he said.

Allen was handcuffed at gunpoint. Police later showed him the AI-captured image that triggered the alert. The crumpled Doritos bag in his pocket had been mistaken for a gun.

вҖңIt was mainly like, am I gonna die? Are they going to kill me? вҖңThey showed me the picture, said that looks like a gun, I said, вҖҳno, itвҖҷs chips.вҖҷвҖқ

Student afraid to return to school after AI sends police after him

The AI system behind the incident is part of OmnilertвҖҷs gun detection technology, introduced in Baltimore County Public Schools last year. It scans existing surveillance footage and alerts police in real time when it detects what it believes to be a weapon.

Omnilert later admitted the incident was a вҖңfalse positiveвҖқ but claimed the system вҖңfunctioned as intended,вҖқ saying its purpose is to вҖңprioritize safety and awareness through rapid human verification.вҖқ

Baltimore County Public Schools echoed the companyвҖҷs statement in a letter to parents, offering counseling services to students impacted by the incident.

вҖңWe understand how upsetting this was for the individual that was searched as well as the other students who witnessed the incident,вҖқ the principal wrote. вҖңOur counselors will provide direct support to the students who were involved.вҖқ

Allen says no one from the school has reached out to him personally.

вҖңThey didnвҖҷt apologize. They just told me it was protocol,вҖқ he said. вҖңI was expecting at least somebody to talk to me about it.вҖқ

The teen now says he no longer feels safe going to school.

вҖңIf I eat another bag of chips or drink something, I feel like theyвҖҷre going to come again,вҖқ Allen said.

The case has sparked fresh debate over the reliability of AI surveillance tools and their real-world consequences, especially in schools.

This incident comes as more institutions implement AI technology. Earlier this month, Major General William вҖҳHankвҖҷ Taylor, one of the top officers in the US Army, admitted to using ChatGPT to make key military decisions.

Meanwhile, the UK introduced strict age verification measures for mature content, requiring users to pass a facial scan to prove theyвҖҷre over 18. This has left some adults unable to access content, such as BritainвҖҷs most tattooed man, who said the age check system told him to вҖңremove his faceвҖқ because it interpreted his tattoos as a mask.

In Baltimore County, an AI-operated security system mistook a high school student's bag of Doritos for a firearm, leading to the studentвҖҷs handcuffing and search. The incident casts a skeptical light on current automated surveillance reliability while underscoring the absurdities of overzealous AI in high-stakes scenarios.

Reports confirm that OpenAI is developing an AI-powered music generator designed to craft original compositions from text and audio prompts. The innovation intensifies competition in creative AI as legal and copyright complexities bubble beneath the surface, leaving both industry pundits and artists raising an ironically skeptical eyebrow.

Microsoft is set to rewrite gaming history with the long-awaited Halo: Campaign Evolved debut on PlayStation 5. Celebrating the franchiseвҖҷs 25th anniversary, the remake marks a decisive shift away from decades of console exclusivity. The move promises to upend industry norms as Microsoft steps away from its traditional gaming stronghold.

FoxconnвҖҷs Hyderabad facility is spearheading a major expansion to double its monthly AirPods production and boost its workforce as part of AppleвҖҷs strategy to diversify manufacturing. The expansion highlights AppleвҖҷs efforts to counter supply chain issues while leveraging IndiaвҖҷs growing market potential.

A fresh set of AI browsers has debuted with OpenAIвҖҷs Atlas browser under the spotlight. One review offers a skeptical take on whether the web really needs a chatbot tour guide, while concurrent coverage highlights emerging security risks. The narrative leaves users both intrigued and wary about these nextвҖҗgeneration productivity tools.

In the latest twist of Apple speculation, the upcoming iPhone 18 series might not only pack 50% more RAM than its predecessor but also boast full 5G satellite connectivity, possibly transforming mobile communication. It's almost as if Apple is trying to bridge the gap between smartphones and space travel.

In a move that has international analysts raising eyebrows and chuckling at the theatrics, President Trump abruptly halted trade negotiations with Canada. Citing tariff grievances and fueled by a series of fiery social media posts, the cancellation underscores the unpredictable drama in todayвҖҷs high-stakes trade environment.

items.title: New updates and more access to Google Earth AI
items.author: diogenico
items.content:
When disasters strike, Google products like Search and Maps help billions of people make critical decisions to stay safe. Our flood forecasting information вҖ” now covering more than two billion people вҖ” provides life-saving forecasts before the most significant river floods. It's helped organizations like World Vision get drinking water and food to communities when they need it most. And during the devastating 2025 California wildfires, we provided crisis alerts with information from local authorities to 15 million people across Los Angeles while showing them where to find shelter in Google Maps. This is all made possible by our geospatial AI models, not only for floods and wildfires, but cyclones, air quality and many more.

We recently introduced Google Earth AI, bringing together these geospatial models to help tackle the planet's most critical needs. Earth AI is built on decades modeling the world, combined with state of the art predictive models and GeminiвҖҷs advanced reasoning, letting enterprises, cities and nonprofits achieve deeper understanding in minutes вҖ” efforts that previously required complex analytics and years of research.

Connecting the dots with Geospatial Reasoning

To solve a complex problem, you need to see the whole picture, not just one piece of it. ThatвҖҷs the idea behind Geospatial Reasoning, a framework powered by Gemini that now lets AI automatically connect different Earth AI models вҖ” like weather forecasts, population maps and satellite imagery вҖ” to answer complex questions.

Instead of just seeing where a storm might hit, our latest research demonstrates that analysts can use Geospatial Reasoning to identify which communities are most vulnerable and what infrastructure is at risk, all at once. For example, Geospatial Reasoning empowers the nonprofit GiveDirectly to respond to disasters by combining flood and population density information, helping them identify who needs direct aid most.

Sign up for consideration to become a Trusted Tester for Geospatial Reasoning. Social impact organizations can learn about future support for nonprofits through Google.org and our non-commercial access programs.

items.title: Claude Memory
items.author: doppp
items.content:
• Expanding to Pro and Max plans

Whether youвҖҷre iterating on a strategy proposal, debugging an issue, or managing multiple projects, Claude picks up right where you left off. Like Team and Enterprise users, you get project-scoped memory (each project has its own separate memory), full control to view and edit what Claude remembers, and incognito chat for conversations that donвҖҷt save to memory.

Before this rollout, we ran extensive safety testing across sensitive wellbeing-related topics and edge casesвҖ”including whether memory could reinforce harmful patterns in conversations, lead to over-accommodation, and enable attempts to bypass our safeguards. Through this testing, we identified areas where Claude's responses needed refinement and made targeted adjustments to how memory functions. These iterations helped us build and improve the memory feature in a way that allows Claude to provide helpful and safe responses to users.

To get started, enable memory in Settings.

Today, weвҖҷre introducing memory to the Claude app, where Claude remembers you and your teamвҖҷs projects and preferences, eliminating the need to re-explain context and keeping complex work moving forward.

Memory is , with granular user controls that help you manage what Claude remembers. WeвҖҷre also introducing Incognito chats that donвҖҷt appear in your conversation history or save to memory.

Memory is rolling out to Team and Enterprise plan users starting today. Enterprise admins can choose whether to disable memory for their organization at any time. Incognito chat is available to all Claude users.

With memory, Claude focuses on learning your professional context and work patterns to maximize productivity. It remembers your teamвҖҷs processes, client needs, project details, and priorities. Sales teams keep client context across deals, product teams maintain specifications across sprints, and executives track initiatives without constantly rebuilding context.

If you use projects, Claude creates a separate memory for each project. This ensures that your product launch planning stays separate from client work, and confidential discussions remain separate from general operations. These project boundaries help you and your teams manage complex, concurrent initiatives without mixing unrelated details, serving as a safety guardrail that keeps sensitive conversations contained.

Claude uses a memory summary to capture all its memories in one place for you to view and edit. In your settings, you can see exactly what Claude remembers from your conversations, and update the summary at any time by chatting with Claude. Based on what you tell Claude to focus on or to ignore, Claude will adjust the memories it references.

Sometimes you need ClaudeвҖҷs help without using or adding to memory. Incognito chat gives you a clean slate for conversations that you donвҖҷt want to preserve in memory. It is perfect for sensitive brainstorming, confidential strategy discussions, or when you simply want a fresh conversation without context from previous chats. Your regular memory and conversation history remain untouched. If youвҖҷre using memory on a Team or Enterprise plan, your standard data retention settings apply.

Starting with teams at work

Memory introduces new safety considerations and we've designed the feature to be useful in work settings, while avoiding sensitive conversations and topics. We're also taking a thoughtful phased approach to ensure these powerful capabilities are deployed responsibly, and will continue to evaluate and test how memory works across the different ways people use Claude before expanding availability.

To see memory in action, enable the feature in Settings, and let Claude generate memory with your past chats at initial set-up. Ask Claude questions like вҖңwhat were we working on last week?вҖқ to see what Claude remembers across your existing chats and connected tools. If you would like to bring your memory details over from a different AI tool or export your memory from Claude for backup or migration, you can follow these instructions.

Great work builds over time. With memory, each conversation with Claude improves the next.

items.title: US hits $38T in debt. Fastest accumulation of $1T outside pandemic
items.author: testing22321
items.content:
WASHINGTON (AP) вҖ” In the midst of a federal government shutdown, the U.S. governmentвҖҷs gross national debt surpassed $38 trillion Wednesday, a record number that highlights the accelerating accumulation of debt on AmericaвҖҷs balance sheet.

ItвҖҷs also the fastest accumulation of a trillion dollars in debt outside of the COVID-19 pandemic вҖ” the national debt in August this year.

Kent Smetters of the University of PennsylvaniaвҖҷs Penn Wharton Budget Model, who served in President George W. BushвҖҷs Treasury Department, told The Associated Press that a growing debt load over time leads ultimately to higher inflation, eroding AmericansвҖҷ purchasing power.

The outlines some of the impacts of rising government debt on Americans вҖ” including higher borrowing costs for things like mortgages and cars, lower wages from businesses having less money available to invest, and more expensive goods and services.

вҖңI think a lot of people want to know that their kids and grandkids are going to be in good, decent shape in the future вҖ” that they will be able to afford a house,вҖқ Smetters said. вҖңThat additional inflation compoundsвҖқ and erodes consumersвҖҷ purchasing power, he said, making it less possible for future generations to achieve home ownership goals.

The Trump administration says its policies are helping to slow government spending and will shrink the nationвҖҷs massive deficit. A new analysis by Treasury Department officials states that from April to September, the cumulative deficit totaled $468 billion. In a post on X Wednesday, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said thatвҖҷs the lowest reading since 2019.

вҖңDuring his first eight months in office, President Trump has reduced the deficit by $350 billion compared to the same period in 2024 by cutting spending and boosting revenue,вҖқ White House spokesman Kush Desai said in a statement, adding that the administration would pursue robust economic growth, lower inflation, tariff revenue, lower borrowing costs and cuts to waste, fraud and abuse.

The Joint Economic Committee that the total national debt has grown by $69,713.82 per second for the past year.

Michael Peterson, chair and CEO of the Peter G. Peterson Foundation, said in a statement that вҖңreaching $38 trillion in debt during a government shutdown is the latest troubling sign that lawmakers are not meeting their basic fiscal duties.вҖқ

вҖңAlong with increasing debt, you get higher interest costs, which are now the fastest growing part of the budget,вҖқ Peterson added. вҖңWe spent $4 trillion on interest over the last decade, but will spend $14 trillion in the next ten years. Interest costs crowd out important public and private investments in our future, harming the economy for every American.вҖқ

The U.S. hit $34 trillion in debt in January 2024, $35 trillion in July 2024 and $36 trillion in November 2024.

items.title: US axes website for reporting human rights abuses by US-armed foreign forces
items.author: tartoran
items.content:
The US State Department has removed an online portal for reporting alleged human rights violations by foreign military units supplied with American weapons.

The Human Rights Reporting Gateway (HRG) acted as a formal "tip line" to the US government.

It was the only publicly accessible channel of its kind for organisations or individuals to inform it directly of potentially serious abuses by US-armed foreign forces.

Its deletion has been condemned by human rights campaigners and by a senior congressional aide who drafted the law requiring it. The State Department insisted it was still abiding by the law.

The portal was established in 2022 following pressure on successive administrations to abide by updated provisions of the Leahy Law, named after former US Senator Patrick Leahy. These require the government to "facilitate receipt" of information on alleged gross violations of human rights by military units supplied by Washington.

Among the cases submitted via the HRG was the alleged excessive use of force by security forces during anti-government protests in Colombia, while several cases were due to be submitted relating to US-armed units of the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) in the occupied West Bank, according to Amnesty International.

Tim Rieser, former senior aide to Senator Leahy who wrote the 2011 amendment mandating information gathering, told the BBC the gateway's removal meant the State Department was "clearly ignoring the law".

He added it was a further sign that "the entire human rights architecture" within the department was being "rendered largely ineffective".

"The United States will find itself supporting foreign security forces that commit heinous crimes even though nothing is done about it," said Mr Rieser. "As a result there will be less incentive for foreign governments to bring people who commit such crimes to justice."

In response, the US State Department insisted it was continuing to receive reports regarding gross violations of human rights and was engaging with "credible organisations" on a full spectrum of human rights concerns. It said: "The Department abides by its legal requirements".

Secretary of State Marco Rubio has overseen a sweeping reorganisation of the State Department involving widespread layoffs and eliminating some offices focused on human rights monitoring. The department this year released a slimmed-down annual human rights report which critics said omitted alleged crimes by US allies but included those by foreign leaders the Trump administration opposes.

The department has previously said its restructure made it leaner and more efficient and followed President Trump's "America First" foreign policy which sees some human rights promotion as ideologically driven.

Charles Blaha, former Director of the Office of Security and Human Rights at the State Department, said people in the field would now have "no established channel" for reporting gross violations of human rights by foreign security forces.

The government's ability to deter abuses was "severely weakened", said Mr Blaha who is now an adviser to the Washington-based think tank Democracy for the Arab World Now (DAWN).

The BBC learned during the summer that the HRG site was earmarked for deletion. Its removal was highlighted by DAWN in August but has not previously been acknowledged by the State Department. The BBC confirmed this week that officials phased out the reporting channel during the department's restructure.

Screengrabs of the portal taken by the BBC before its deletion show it asked for information on US-armed foreign military units involved in alleged abuses including extrajudicial killings, forced disappearances, torture and rape. It required extensive details to ensure credible reporting including unit names, identities of alleged perpetrators, specific locations and dates.

Blaha had already voiced frustration that despite the HRG passing its pilot phase, the Biden administration had not done enough to publicise it, meaning the provision to "facilitate receipt" of information was still not being fully honoured before the Trump administration deleted the channel entirely.

The US is the world's biggest contributor of military aid to foreign countries, supplying funding, equipment, training and arms to more than 150 nations. Its vetting procedures to prevent arms going to units involved in breaches of international law have evolved over the years, with the 1997 legislation named after Senator Leahy being one its major pillars.

At the heart of the law is Congress sending a message to the administration that taxpayer funding cannot support human rights abuses abroad, says Amanda Klasing, who oversees government relations at Amnesty International USA.

"If I'm a member of Congress, my perspective is: 'I want to protect my constituents and their taxpayer funding from going to torturers or people that kill their own citizens for protesting," she told the BBC.

Klasing oversaw the compilation and submission of several reports to the Human Rights Reporting Gateway.

These included allegations that American arms were used by Colombian security forces during mass anti-government protests in 2021, in which at least 47 people were killed and many wounded according to the group. Amnesty says among the arms were US-supplied high-capacity launchers for stun and smoke grenades.

Klasing says she had also gathered evidence relating to the killing of 20 Palestinians during IDF raids in occupied East Jerusalem and the West Bank in October and November 2023. Amnesty was preparing to submit its report on these incidents to the HRG before its deletion.

items.title: Context engineering is sleeping on the humble hyperlink
items.author: mbleigh
items.content:
As we all learn more about Context Engineering for LLMs (see AnthropicвҖҷs post for an excellent primer), weвҖҷve identified a few important limitations. Conversations should be append-only to maximize cacheability. Models are typically more responsive to вҖңfreshвҖқ context close to the end of the window. Models typically perform worse when overwhelmed with large amounts of context.

With this in mind, a key tension comes into focus: the model needs access to all valuable context, when that context is relevant to the task at hand.

Context engineering is effectively the practice of finding ways to manage this tension. Popular solutions include:

  • Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), which attempts to dynamically discover and load specific relevant context for the current query proactively.
  • , which encapsulate specialized instructions and tools to avoid polluting the main thread.
  • , which allow the model to proactively request information that it deems relevant using tool calls.

ThereвҖҷs one technique that I feel is woefully underutilized by agents today: the humble hyperlink.

The obligatory human analogy

If you, a human, need to learn something an LLM (letвҖҷs say something about an open source library), you will probably follow a trajectory that looks something like the following:

  • Do a Google search for the topic you need to understand
  • Click a relevant link to e.g. a docs page, read a high-level guide
  • Depending on your needs, maybe Cmd+Click a few more pages or the reference docs to open them in new tabs to review
  • Refer between your various open tabs as you complete your task

Once you found an through search, you were able to explore the topic through discovered , filling your mental context with relevant information.

We can do the same thing with LLMs.

HATEOAS in the era of Agents

The power of linked data is nothing new. Folks who have been building HTTP APIs for a long time might be familiar with HATEOAS, or вҖңHypertext as the Engine of Application StateвҖқ. Purists have long claimed that a вҖңtrulyвҖқ RESTful API should be fully self-describing, such that a client can explore and interact with it knowing nothing but an entrypoint in advance, with hyperlinks providing all necessary context to discover and consume additional endpoints.

This never worked in practice. Building hypertext APIs was too cumbersome and to actually consume APIs a human needed to understand the API structure in a useful manner anyway. So it was more useful just to have a вҖңREST-ishвҖқ API and a good documentation page that humans could use. Creating вҖңmachine-readableвҖқ hyperlinked APIs that machines could navigate in theory but not in practice just wasnвҖҷt practical. LLMs change this dramatically.

When the machine can not only parse but also the context and relevance of hyperlinks you have an actually useful paradigm: Hypertext as the Engine of State.

This does apply to the web and HTTP APIs вҖ” I expect in the next few years weвҖҷll see a resurgence of hypermedia concepts to make APIs more self-documenting (вҖңdump the entire API schema as OpenAPIвҖқ is a start but not really sufficient).

But it also applies to local data, agent-specific data, really data we want an agent to be able to discover and read.

So, how do we make all of our context linkable for agents?

The scaffolding required to implement a powerful link-based context system is lightweight enough to be trivial. You need only:

  1. A that accepts a list of URIs as arguments.
  2. An that brings at least one URI into context.

HereвҖҷs a demonstrative example using Genkit in JS that uses system instructions as an entrypoint.

If we run the above code with a few different prompts, we see links in action:

The results are exactly what weвҖҷd hope:

  • In the first test, no link reading was required and no links were read. The answer was right in the system prompt!
  • In the second test, reading one link was sufficient to reach a conclusion.
  • In the final test, the model understood to fetch context linked from the first loaded document to reach a conclusion.

So, in ~30 lines of code with very little prompting and while using a cost-effective model, weвҖҷve created a system that can dynamically load and correctly apply relevant context on-demand.

Links are a powerful tool in the context engineering toolbelt because of their , , and .

  • Links are trivial to implement, and current models are вҖңintuitivelyвҖқ good at understanding how to follow links.
  • Links can surface in the flow of a conversation. They can be specified in a system prompt, provided by the user, or returned by a tool.
  • Links are because they use a small number of tokens to provide to specific information. The model can load a link if it needs it but if it doesnвҖҷt few tokens are wasted.
  • Links are because they consolidate many types of reads into a single tool. You can have a link that dynamically loads information about the current user, a link that loads a local file, and a link that returns static instructions. You donвҖҷt need a separate tool for each type of data.
  • Links provide mitigating issues of context rot and recency bias in models. Because linked context is loaded when itвҖҷs needed by the model the context is вҖңfresherвҖқ instead of overloading a system prompt.

MCP Resources: The future is now(ish)

To make link-based context engineering a universal feature, we need a way to provide the linked content to the model. Many agents (and some model APIs) have built in various forms of вҖңfetch URLвҖқ or вҖңsearch the webвҖқ tools that can automatically fetch data from public sources. But the content we want to link might not always be available on the public internet.

The good news is we already have the exact primitive we need to solve this problem: MCP Resources. Resources allows servers to register URIs (or patterns of URIs) that can then be read on-demand by clients to provide static or dynamic content. Sounds perfect, right?

The bad news is IвҖҷm not aware of a single MCP client that makes MCP resources consumable by the . Today, they only allow resources to be inserted via @-mentions and similar devices. This doesnвҖҷt enable linking. . We just need one critical thing:

Every MCP-enabled agent should expose a tool that accepts one or more URIs and aggregates reading across all connected MCP servers (and probably web URLs as well).

There are other changes that would help: a mechanism of indexing / listing available MCP resources and exposing that in the system instructions, maybe even indexing MCP resources so that they can be searched using RAG techniques. But just enabling agents to access linked content opens up a world of new context engineering techniques.

Working with what you have

If youвҖҷre building your own agent, you donвҖҷt need any new technology to start making linked context вҖ” you can do it in a few dozen lines of code like I demonstrated above. But even if youвҖҷre trying to integrate with existing agents like Gemini CLI, Claude Code, or Cursor, you can still build with linked data patterns.

The Firebase MCP Server recently launched new capabilities including a slash command for setting up Firebase in a project. We had some specific and pretty complex use cases in mind, so we built linked context into the MCP server itself:

  1. We added support for MCP Resources to the Firebase MCP Server.
  2. We created a read_resources MCP tool that was capable of reading resources (from the Firebase MCP Server only).
  3. We created an MCP prompt that creates a guided workflow to walk the model step-by-step through configuring Firebase, including linked branching paths.

WeвҖҷve tested out this initialization flow against several popular coding agents that support MCP Prompts вҖ” each of them is able to understand and follow hyperlinks using our MCP serverвҖҷs tool and weвҖҷve made onboarding to Firebase all the easier for it.

Effective context engineering is constantly evolving as models and agent harnesses improve; however, hyperlinks are such a powerfully efficient mechanism for information traversal that I canвҖҷt imagine a future of agents that include linked context.

The next time youвҖҷre thinking of building half a dozen or tools for your agent, take a step back and consider: could the humble hyperlink get the job done instead?

items.title: I spent a year making an ASN.1 compiler in D
items.author: BradleyChatha
items.content:
Published: 2025/10/23 | Updated: 2025/10/23

вҖҰ and itвҖҷs still nowhere near complete.

In this post IвҖҷll just rambling about ASN.1; parts of the compiler implementation, and some of the toolвҖҷs output rather than the tool itself as its still too WIP to really advertise on its own yet.

This post is unstructured, so you can just pick somewhere random and start reading from there with no/minimal context lost.

Note: the name of the tool is dasn1.

IвҖҷm currently writing Juptune - a toy async I/O framework that attempts to implement as much of its stack as possible in pure D.

IвҖҷm really interested in writing an implementation of TLS, which means I need to be able to handle x.509 certificates (i.e. TLS/SSL certs), which means I need to be able to handle their underlying data encoding: ASN.1вҖҷs DER encoding.

So basically I just wanted to do this at the end of the day, nothing much deeper than that. IвҖҷve never written or worked on a proper compiler project before that wasnвҖҷt toy-sized so I saw a ton of growth potentialвҖҰ the main thing thatвҖҷs grown however is the mental scar ASN.1вҖҷs left on me.

IвҖҷve succesfully generated code that can parse a couple of x.509 certificates IвҖҷve thrown at it, and IвҖҷve started work on an almost-D-native (excluding crypto primitives) implementation of TLS 1.3.

IвҖҷm constantly amazed about how much of modern life relies on these ancient, overly complicated specs from the 90s. ASN.1 is used in some form or another and yet I bet youвҖҷve never even heard of it before, just have a look on wikipedia.

briefly - what is ASN.1?

ASN.1 is the result of a bunch of graybeards from the late 80s+ trying to design an overengineered data specification language. In other words, itвҖҷs protobuf on steroids.

ThereвҖҷs two parts of ASN.1: ThereвҖҷs the ASN.1 notation (defined by x.680, x.681, x.682, and x.683), and then thereвҖҷs the various encodings (BER, CER, DER, PER, XER, JERвҖҰ). In this post IвҖҷll mainly be focusing on the notation + DER.

Similarly to protobuf you use the notation to define a structured way to represent data, and then use tooling that can generate encoders/decoders for a specific encoding, in a specific programming language.

HereвҖҷs a choice snippet of the ASN.1 notation for RFC 5280 (which defines whatвҖҷs commonly known as TLS certificates):

Encoding wise hereвҖҷs a quick of some of the more well known ones:

  • BER - Basic binary encoding rules. Uses a Tag-Length-Value (TLV) format that supports functionally infinite lengths of data.
  • CER - A limited subset of BER where each value can only have one possible encoding. It uses some odd design choices (such as using the infinite-length forms of encoded data), so no one really uses it.
  • DER - A limited subset of BER where each value can only have one possible encoding, but with a more sane choice of decisions made compared to CER. This is used extensively for cryptographic purposes.
  • PER - A binary encoding that uses contraint information to encode data in the absolute minimum amount of bits possible. ThereвҖҷs also like 4 variations of this one btw.
  • OER - Similar to PER except it keeps the bytes of values separate, whereas PER can pack the bits of different values together tightly.
  • XER - An XML based encoding. The ASN.1 notation grammar actually has XML-specific parts to it just for this encoding (because of course it does).
  • JER - A JSON based encoding.

Did I ever mention that ASN.1 is complicated? On the one hand the sheer amount of possible encodings is daunting, but on the other hand it shows a certain flexbility that ASN.1 provides - you could even invent your own domain-specific encoding if needed.

Loosely speaking you can define ASN.1вҖҷs notation as being the вҖңbaseвҖқ notation defined in x.680, with the sometimes-optional addon specifications defined in x.681, x.682, x.683.

These specifications are also written in academicese so for mere uneducated mortals such as myself, simply trying to read and understand what the specifications are saying in the first place is already a large hurdle. I IвҖҷve started to get the hang of it though.

Fortunately for my use case of handling x.509 certificates, thereвҖҷs no hard requirement for anything beyond x.680 and so x.680 is the only spec IвҖҷve attempted to implement so far (outside of x.690 which describes how BER/CER/DER works - which is actually a to read compared to the x.68x specs).

x.680 isnвҖҷt the worst thing in the world to implement, itвҖҷs just the fact that thereвҖҷs a more to it than youвҖҷd think from a quick glance at a code example, as well as some relatively annoying вҖңtransformationвҖқ (semantic) rules you have to acccount for.

Generally though IвҖҷd say the really difficult parts seem to come from its extensions.

x.680 woes - historical deprecations

One of the more annoying parts of implementing a parser for ASN.1вҖҷs notation is that x.680 has been revised several times over the years, which includes the deprecation + removal of certain features.

And so some other specifications you read through will either:

  1. Use older forms of syntax that are no longer recommended/supported.
  2. Replace the older forms of syntax with newer variants which can be more complicated to implement.

Meaning that if you want to write a compiler for ASN.1 for a specific use case, but want it to also be an implementation of the more modern specsвҖҰ then youвҖҷll have to partially implement/hack around some of the older stuff thatвҖҷs no longer defined in the up to date spec documentation.

An example would be the syntax, which I have a separate section on.

This is essentially the academic equivalent of an Elder Scroll - you will go insane attempting to read let alone mentally parse this damn thing.

x.681 describes the Information Class Object system. IвҖҷd to talk to you more about it more in depth but I havenвҖҷt put in enough effort to confidently state much about how it works.

One of the few parts I sort of understand and can talk about is that x.681 has a really cool feature where Information Classes can be given a custom initialisation syntax:

IвҖҷd absolutely love to attempt to implement x.681 for the challenge of this feature alone, however I only have so much energy (and sanity), so itвҖҷll likely be a while until I even properly consider it.

x.682 describes the Table Constraint feature. IвҖҷm going to be honest I donвҖҷt understand a single thing about this feature - I took one look at the specification and was like вҖңabsolutely notвҖқ.

x.683 describes the ability to create templated (sorry, вҖңparameterisedвҖқ) types. Similar to the other ASN.1 extensions I havenвҖҷt looked much into this feature, but it appears to be a lot simpler to implement than the others.

In essence, one of the things you can do is this:

It supports values as well as types within its template parameters (similarly to D!) so thereвҖҷs a few cool things you can do with it I guess.

ASN.1вҖҷs notation is also pretty cool!

Despite the many, , pains of this god forsaken technology, itвҖҷs actually really interesting and powerful at the same time.

ASN.1вҖҷs constraint system

ASN.1вҖҷs notation contains a pretty neat feature where you can add special constraints onto types + fields. So rather than having a stray вҖңProtcolPacket.field1.field2.xyz MUST be between 0 and 2вҖқ thatвҖҷs super easy to miss, you can instead describe this constraint within ASN.1 itself which (good) tooling will then take into account for you.

HereвҖҷs some examples of the simpler constraints available:

ThereвҖҷs a few more constraints available butвҖҰ theyвҖҷre mostly pretty complex ones that I donвҖҷt want to have to think about.

ItвҖҷs really cool to see that ASN.1 has a feature like this though, considering the only other langauge IвҖҷve personally encountered that has a similar feature is Ada.

ASN.1вҖҷs versioning system

ASN.1 generally uses the type in order to, well, identify specific things, e.g. extensions found within x.509 certificates.

s are also used to provide versions to modules, for example:

Everything between the curly brackets is an OBJECT IDENTIFIER for this exact module - technically no other ASN.1 module in existance should ever use this specific OBJECT IDENTIFIER. The optional labels (e.g. ) have no meaning beyond aiding human comprehension, itвҖҷs the values (e.g. ) that are actually used to create the identifier.

As a great example of this versioning system, it just so happens that this specific module has a more modern version that has this specific OBJECT IDENTIFIER instead:

This updated version doesnвҖҷt change how data is encoded to/from DER but instead it simply uses more modern syntax and features.

This is important because older specifications will be using whereas newer ones will likely be using instead, and so there needs to be a more clear-cut way to distinguish between these two versions of the module other than going by its name - and this is where OBJECT IDENTIFIERS come in handy.

When importing modules within ASN.1 notation you can (and should) specify an OBJECT IDENTIFIER as well:

Maybe IвҖҷm just a nerd, but I find this to almost be a thing of beauty with how simple yet effective it is.

D is easy to generate code for

D has several quality of life features that makes it surprisingly easy to generate code for - features that would definitely make the compiler more annoying to work with when targeting other languages.

These features on their own arenвҖҷt exactly rare to see, but the specific combination is what makes everything work together so well.

Static imports & fully qualified names

in D means вҖңimport this module, but ONLY allow it to be used via its fully qualified nameвҖқ:

You can even override the module name, as strange as that sounds!

This feature is a godsend for preserving the original names of ASN.1 types. For example, Juptune provides an error type called which comes from the module.

Without static imports IвҖҷd have to be careful of ASN.1 code that defines a type as itвҖҷd otherwise come into conflict with JuptuneвҖҷs own type.

However, with static imports, I can basically just generate code that looks like this:

Completely removing the need of me having to worry about symbol name conflicts.

On a similar vein D allows you to specify that instead of looking up a symbol from any available symbol table (e.g. local vars; non-static imports, etc.) it should instead perform a lookup using the current moduleвҖҷs top-level symbols.

The leading in is what causes the module-local lookup.

Essentially, this feature compliments the static import feature to help make it much harder for ASN.1 types to accidentally refer to the wrong symbol when converted into D code.

In short: this feature allowed me to be lazy with certain parts of the compiler :D

As the name suggests, allows you to retrieve the type of any particular symbol you pass into it - this is great when dealing with code generation since sometimes it can be to structure your code in a way where you can easily preserve the type name of some symbol youвҖҷre working with.

In other words вҖңthis letвҖҷs me write bad code and make it still workвҖқ.

First example is around how some getters and setters for SEQEUENCE fields are generated. Instead of doing the correct thing and preserving the type name for each field, I got lazy and just used :

The second example is around error messages. Instead of needing to keep track of the current typeвҖҷs name when generating error messagesвҖҰ I could just use to get the type instead:

WhatвҖҷs is that because the entire string is composed of compile-time constants, it doesnвҖҷt actually require an allocation + concat at runtime since the compiler will constant fold it for you. This allows to still be marked as !

D allows trailing commas in almost every context

Generating a parameter list and donвҖҷt want to have to care about whether thereвҖҷs an extra comma or not?

DвҖҷs got your back! (Except for specifying multiple modules in a single import statement, then for some reason youвҖҷre not allowed, but shh about that).

For a while a lot of the types being generated (and some of the core decoding types) didnвҖҷt have a implementation. ThisвҖҷd normally mean that I couldnвҖҷt just use willy-nilly but instead the compiler would need knowledge about which types had a or not.

However, as is the common theme now D allows us to be very lazy - instead of keeping track of this ourselves in dasn1, we can instead just generate code where itвҖҷs the D compilerвҖҷs concern instead of ourвҖҷs:

Job sorted (and future proofed!).

You could definitely utilise DвҖҷs metaprogramming for more complicated stuff, but itвҖҷs also good for silly little things like this.

Interesting D-specific parts of the implementation

Naturally IвҖҷve tried to use whatever D features that I could in order to implement dasn1, so I thought IвҖҷd pick a few parts of the code that rely on DвҖҷs features quite heavily as a small showcase.

Mixin templates for AST nodes

Mixin templates are a fairly quirky feature of D - it allows you to define a normal template (essentially a compile-time collection of symbols) and then copy-paste them wherever you like, whether thatвҖҷs inside a class, struct, the top-level module etc.

Since the ASN.1 grammar only had a handful of node вҖңtypesвҖқ, I decided to use mixin templates to model each specific вҖңtypeвҖқ:

I probably couldвҖҷve gotten away with just using templated base classes instead, but thereвҖҷs a few differences that actually make that kind of annoying. Namely itвҖҷd create some bloated symbol names which would make reading compiler errors even more painful than it already ended up being.

LetвҖҷs look at one of the AST nodes again:

This is a node that contains several other nodes. itself supports an unbounded amount of node types it can store, since D supports variadic template parameters. You may be asking what the API for this even looks like, and IвҖҷll be glad to show you a quick snippet:

LetвҖҷs have a look at a node instead now:

This node has a similar template-based API for most of its operations:

However the main feature of the node is its function. This function requires the user to pass in a handler function for each possible node type that the can store, and this requirement is enforced at compile-time so that changes to the node type list will immediately require all appropriate functions to be updated (i.e. no silent breakage).

This is surprisingly easy to implement with D due to its first-class metaprogramming features, IвҖҷll try my best to be brief with how this all works:

  • is the template parameter containing a compile-time tuple of all possible types that this can store.
  • is a template that generates a new compile-time tuple, where each is mapped into a function pointer type.
  • uses the result of as its main parameter. Since this is a compile-time tuple of types it automagically gets expanded into multiple parameters under the hood.
  • within вҖҷs body allows us to iterate over a compile-time collection (in this case, ) and duplicate the foreachвҖҷs body for each item. In this case, so we can make a statement per item in .
  • If is .
  • results in (Result delegate(Node1), Result delegate(Node2))
  • And вҖҷs parameters expand into match(scope delegate(Node1) handler_0, scope delegate(Node2) handler_1)

that we could use this example match function like so:

I know thatвҖҷs a lot to take in especially since I have to be briefer than usual, but TL;DR D makes the hard stuff easy while still being relatively easy on the eyes. I would make a snarky comparison with C++ but literally no one expects C++ metaprogramming to be readable at this point.

D Snark: The forever-experimental allocator package

10 years ago (October 2015) DвҖҷs standard library was given an experimental package called std.experimental.allocator. It has a pretty neat but kind of janky way of composing a bunch of allocation building blocks together, in order to вҖңeasilyвҖқ make custom allocators.

I use it for the ASN.1 stuff since it makes it easy to construct and dispose classes within code, and it looks kind of cool to boot:

The issue is this package is still experimental 10 years later and I wouldnвҖҷt be surprised if it gets removed sooner or later, especially with the Phobos v2 work thatвҖҷll hopefully exist in some form before I retire (IвҖҷm 26).

:D The sign of someone who loves this damn language is that they canвҖҷt help but provide some level of historical snark. I have no further comments, I just miss the days I had hope for DвҖҷs future xD

alias this - a very occasionally useful feature

Situation: I need to store IR nodes using a base class rather than a specific concrete implementation class, but IвҖҷd still like to limit the potential options without having to go down the SumType route.

Solution: This short but sweet struct (note: this is a different struct for IR purposes, not AST purposes).

We can initialise this struct like so:

Now the fun part comes from this weird line. Normally when working with a wrapper struct like this youвҖҷd have do something like:

With anytime we try to perform an operation (e.g. casting; function calls, etc.) that the struct itself does not support, the compiler will instead try to use it on the field instead:

ItвҖҷs a very weird, niche feature which might even get removed or at least deprecated in the future, but it allows for some mild syntax cleanup as shown above.

Some of the IR types try to strictly limit the way that user code can query and interact with their data, mainly to help prevent potential memory corruptionвҖҰ at least that was my original, flawed reasoning.

This can be when writing unittests, as sometimes you just need to query a very particular part of a typeвҖҷs data without having to go through all of its hurdles.

And so by simply slapping onto a funciton definition, you now have an escape hatch that wonвҖҷt make its way out into real code:

Templates + with() = terse-ish test harnesses

ThereвҖҷs a few examples of this within the codebase. Sometimes unittests are for the most part identical except:

  • They need to tweak a few types here and there.
  • They need an вҖңinitialiserвҖқ function that returns a different type from other unittests.
  • They need to change what the function-to-be-tested is (and thus what some of the types being used are).
  • Sometimes the test case type itself needs to have a few types changed.
  • Everything else is the same though between unittests - itвҖҷs mainly just types getting in the way.

ItвҖҷs one of those things where you kind of just have to use it and do it before you вҖңget itвҖқ, so I apologise for the really poor explanation, but this is essentially something you can do with templates.

HereвҖҷs one of the templated вҖңtest harnessesвҖқ I used - this one in particular is for testing the AST -> IR converter functions.

One main issue, especially for the larger tests, is that specifying (and more minorly ) can start to make the code look chunky and a bit harder to read.

So by using the magical statement, instead of writing and , we can just write and and the compiler will know how to lookup these otherwise missing/undefined symbols:

Again this is one of those things that on paper sounds really stupid (and impossible to easily describe), but grows on you really fast when you give it a try.

While ASN.1вҖҷs basic syntax looks pretty easy from an initial glance, that illusion shatters once you start getting into it more deeply.

ASN.1 has various separate value forms that start with a left bracket (), a lot of these forms are ambiguous due to a variety of factors and can only be distinguished with semantic context.

Given that dans1 has a clean split between syntax and semantic analysis, вҖңthis does not spark joyвҖқ as the kids would say.

IвҖҷll let this comment from the parser code explain itself:

ItвҖҷs hard to find important info in the specs

Example: I canвҖҷt even remember the exact conditions, but I remember having to debug some generated decoder code since it was failing to decode a specific field. It turned out that this field was under certain вҖңexact conditionsвҖқ that meant its tag was supposed to be treated as instead of the module-default .

I still have idea where in the spec this behaviour gets mentioned and so I basically had to wing a fix and hope it works going forward.

More generally this feeling and scenario has happened quite a few times - the information is scattered (sometimes across different specs) and is hard to keep track of.

Another example is around module versions. The spec makes absolutely zero mention (that I can see) on how to version modules for non ISO/ITU purposes, and I would greatly appreciate if anyone could help me find this information.

IвҖҷd be amazed if thereвҖҷs a 100% spec compliant implementation out there, even commercially.

You need to implement constraints 3 separate times

  1. The first implementation is to type-check constraints, e.g. UTF8String (SIZE ("yagababa")) doesnвҖҷt make sense.
  2. The second implementation is to confirm that ASN.1 notation values are correct, e.g. needs to trigger an error.
  3. The third implementation is to generate runtime checks when you generate code from the ASN.1 notation.

ItвҖҷs tedious and not very fun, but thereвҖҷs no real way around it.

For a newbie to compiler programming like me I also found it really hard to deal with useful error messages. I ended up running the checks twice: one time to see if thereвҖҷs even an error at all, and the second time to build up the error string. This is mainly complicated by the existance of UNION and (especially) INTERSECTION constraints.

The dream of immutable IR nodes

I foolishly made the mistake of believing that once I converted the generic AST nodes into the more specific IR nodes that I wouldnвҖҷt have to make any major changes to the underlying data (beyond setting up things like symbol tables).

:D Unfortunately that wonderfully naive thought was quickly crushed as ASN.1 requires the semantic stage to perform certain transformations, certain transformations (e.g. ) that ended up/are going to be really annoying due to the way IвҖҷve structured the code.

But thatвҖҷs future BradвҖҷs problem.

I am thankful that x.509 is an old enough specification that the ASN.1 notation only uses the older syntax of x.680.

The alternative is that youвҖҷd an implementation of the x.681, x.682, and x.683 specs to use any of the newer stuff - this is absolutely non-trivial to implement, and I imagine this is one of the many reasons ASN.1 hasnвҖҷt ever really taken off outside of historical and commercialised spaces.

There is one exception to the above however and that is .

ItвҖҷd basically be used to define a type whoвҖҷs contents could be conditioned by some other field:

You then have to piece together what identifier matches which type. Dasn1 doesnвҖҷt actually implement as-is since even by the 2003 revision it was deprecated.

Instead, for better or for worse, dasn1 has a hacked together intrinsic called :

This essentially gets lowered down into the decoding code for but without any sort of tag validation enabled. Unfortunately until/unless I want to implement Information Object Classes, IвҖҷm then stuck with having to manually call into the decoding code when I want to turn fields into their actual types.

This is more of a personal one.

Between the various different aspects of the ASN.1 compiler, the x.68x specs, the x.690 spec, and all of the other projects building off of this ASN.1 work (x.509 certificate handling, TLS 1.3) I started to feel like a stranger in my own codebase, even just a week after I had last touched it.

ItвҖҷll definitely be interesting making future improvements/changes as my at-hand knowledge is constantly dwindling.

From having to write 20,000 different node visitors for various reasons; to hand-rolling a syntax parser for a boring, drawn out grammar; to needing to write code that looks 95% the same as the last but that last 5% of difference ranges from drudge to mentally taxing, repeated 9000 times.

I think I can finally say I have some proper compiler experience under my belt ;(

But lord knows that each and every milestone has been so extremely rewarding (as long as I try not to think about the fact that almost no one will be using this code).

p.s. DonвҖҷt try to make a template-based parser combinator for the entire grammar of a language you donвҖҷt personally control unless you want to see symbol names that are 10Mb+ long and explode the binary size by over 100Mb. DonвҖҷt ask me how I know.

(I even hard crashed the D compiler I use once, since I guess the error message was literally too long. That endlessly scrolling consoleвҖҰ)

A wasted year of my life later and thereвҖҷs still an insane amount of work left on everything relating to this project (and Juptune) in general, but I think itвҖҷs making me a better programmer. Maybe.

The dream is that one day I can put вҖңmade an ASN.1 compiler + x.509 certificate handler + TLS 1.3 implementationвҖқ on my CV and still get told вҖңsorry, youвҖҷre a good match except you donвҖҷt have 6 months of production experience in Ansible, we canвҖҷt hire youвҖқ by a recruiter. God I love this industry.

DonвҖҷt do ASN.1 kids, youвҖҷll never be the same.

items.title: US probes Waymo robotaxis over school bus safety
items.author: gmays

items.title: Show HN: Deta Surf вҖ“ An open source and local-first AI notebook
items.author: mxek
items.content:
We got frustrated with the fragmented experience of exploring & creating across our file manager, the web and document apps. Lots of manual searching, opening windows & tabs, scrolling, and ultimately copying & pasting into a document editor.

Surf is a desktop app meant for simultaneous research and thinking to minimize the grunt work. ItвҖҷs made of two parts:

1) A multi-media library where you can save and organize files and webpages into collections called Notebooks.

2) A LLM-powered smart document which you can auto-generate using the context from any stored page, tab or entire notebook. This document contains deep links back to the source material вҖ” like a page of a PDF or timestamp in a YouTube video. Unlike Deep Research products (or NotebookLMs chat) the entire thing is editable. The user also stays in the loop.

With a technology like AI, context / data is proving to be king. We think it should stay under the userвҖҷs control, with minimal lock in: where you can own & export, and plug & play with different models. ThatвҖҷs why Surf is:

- Open Source on GitHub - Open (& Local Data): the data saved in Surf is stored on your local machine in open and accessible formats and mostly works offline. - Open Model Choice: you can choose which models you use with Surf, and can add custom & Local LLMs

Early users include students & researchers who are learning and doing thematic research using Surf.

items.title: The game theory of how algorithms can drive up prices
items.author: isaacfrond
items.content:
Imagine a town with two widget merchants. Customers prefer cheaper widgets, so the merchants must compete to set the lowest price. Unhappy with their meager profits, they meet one night in a smoke-filled tavern to discuss a secret plan: If they raise prices together instead of competing, they can both make more money. But that kind of intentional price-fixing, called collusion, has long been illegal. The widget merchants decide not to risk it, and everyone else gets to enjoy cheap widgets.

For well over a century, U.S. law has followed this basic template: Ban those backroom deals, and fair prices should be maintained. These days, itвҖҷs not so simple. Across broad swaths of the economy, sellers increasingly rely on computer programs called learning algorithms, which repeatedly adjust prices in response to new data about the state of the market. These are often much simpler than the вҖңdeep learningвҖқ algorithms that power modern artificial intelligence, but they can still be prone to unexpected behavior.

So how can regulators ensure that algorithms set fair prices? Their traditional approach wonвҖҷt work, as it relies on finding explicit collusion. вҖңThe algorithms definitely are not having drinks with each other,вҖқ said Aaron Roth, a computer scientist at the University of Pennsylvania.

Yet a widely cited 2019 paper showed that algorithms could learn to collude tacitly, even when they werenвҖҷt programmed to do so. A team of researchers pitted two copies of a simple learning algorithm against each other in a simulated market, then let them explore different strategies for increasing their profits. Over time, each algorithm learned through trial and error to retaliate when the other cut prices вҖ” dropping its own price by some huge, disproportionate amount. The end result was high prices, backed up by mutual threat of a price war.

Implicit threats like this also underpin many cases of human collusion. So if you want to guarantee fair prices, why not just require sellers to use algorithms that are inherently incapable of expressing threats?

In a recent paper, Roth and four other computer scientists showed why this may not be enough. They proved that even seemingly benign algorithms that optimize for their own profit can sometimes yield bad outcomes for buyers. вҖңYou can still get high prices in ways that kind of look reasonable from the outside,вҖқ said Natalie Collina, a graduate student working with Roth who co-authored the new study.

Researchers donвҖҷt all agree on the implications of the finding вҖ” a lot hinges on how you define вҖңreasonable.вҖқ But it reveals how subtle the questions around algorithmic pricing can get, and how hard it may be to regulate.

вҖңWithout some notion of a threat or an agreement, itвҖҷs very hard for a regulator to come in and say, вҖҳThese prices feel wrong,вҖҷвҖқ said Mallesh Pai, an economist at Rice University. вҖңThatвҖҷs one reason why I think this paper is important.вҖқ

The recent paper studies algorithmic pricing through the lens of game theory, an interdisciplinary field at the border of economics and computer science that analyzes the mathematics of strategic competitions. ItвҖҷs one way to explore the failures of pricing algorithms in a controlled setting.

вҖңWhat weвҖҷre trying to do is create collusion in the lab,вҖқ said Joseph Harrington, a University of Pennsylvania economist who wrote an influential review paper on regulating algorithmic collusion and was not involved in the new research. вҖңOnce we do so, we want to figure out how to destroy collusion.вҖқ

To understand the key ideas, it helps to start with the simple game of rock-paper-scissors. A learning algorithm, in this context, can be any strategy that a player uses to choose a move in each round based on data from previous rounds. Players might try out different strategies over the course of the game. But if theyвҖҷre playing well, theyвҖҷll ultimately converge to a state that game theorists call equilibrium. In equilibrium, each playerвҖҷs strategy is the best possible response to the otherвҖҷs strategy, so neither player has an incentive to change.

In rock-paper-scissors, the ideal strategy is simple: You should play a random move each round, choosing all three possibilities equally often. Learning algorithms shine if one player takes a different approach. In that case, choosing moves based on previous rounds can help the other player win more often than if they just played randomly.

Suppose, for instance, that after many rounds you realize that your opponent, a geologist, chose rock more than 50% of the time. If youвҖҷd played paper every round, you would have won more often. Game theorists refer to this painful realization as regret.

Researchers have devised simple learning algorithms that are always guaranteed to leave you with zero regret. Slightly more sophisticated learning algorithms called вҖңno-swap-regretвҖқ algorithms also guarantee that whatever your opponent did, you couldnвҖҷt have done better by swapping all instances of any move with any other move (say, by playing paper every time you actually played scissors). In 2000, game theorists proved that if you pit two no-swap-regret algorithms against each other in any game, theyвҖҷll end up in a specific kind of equilibrium вҖ” one that would be the optimal equilibrium if they only played a single round. ThatвҖҷs an attractive property, because single-round games are much simpler than multi-round ones. In particular, threats donвҖҷt work because players canвҖҷt follow through.

In a 2024 paper, Jason Hartline, a computer scientist at Northwestern University, and two graduate students translated the classic results from the 2000 paper to a model of a competitive market, where players can set new prices every round. In that context, the results implied that dueling no-swap-regret algorithms would always end up with competitive prices when they reached equilibrium. Collusion was impossible.

However, no-swap-regret algorithms arenвҖҷt the only pricing game strategies in the world of online marketplaces. So what happens when a no-swap-regret algorithm faces a different benign-looking opponent?

According to game theorists, the best strategy to play against a no-swap-regret algorithm is simple: Start with a specific probability for each possible move, and then choose one move at random every round, no matter what your opponent does. The ideal assignment of probabilities for this вҖңnonresponsiveвҖқ approach depends on the specific game youвҖҷre playing.

In the summer of 2024, Collina and her colleague Eshwar Arunachaleswaran set out to find those optimal probabilities for a two-player pricing game. They found that the best strategy assigned strikingly high probabilities to very high prices, along with lower probabilities for a wide range of lower prices. If youвҖҷre playing against a no-swap-regret algorithm, this strange strategy will maximize your profit. вҖңTo me, it was a complete surprise,вҖқ Arunachaleswaran said.

items.title: C64 Blood Money
items.author: mariuz
items.content:
While I still remember the details, I thought IвҖҷd go over my port of to the . It had some really cool bits in it, and I had great fun doing it. Some parts make me cringe a little now, so itвҖҷd be interesting to revive it, and see if I can get it building again вҖ“ or at the very least, go over the code againвҖҰ

One thing that DID bug me, even back then, was the crappy starfield. It was just some characters stuck in the map, and it sucked.

So what were the main components?

  1. Multi-Directional scrolling

These are the major parts and weвҖҷll take them one at a time. Before that, I want to briefly look over the code, and talk about the devkit вҖ“ . Programmers Development System. This was a lovely system, and one IвҖҷm sad I didnвҖҷt find while raiding the Offices.

The system consisted of a card, and a cartridge. The ISA card was common for all systems, while the target cartridge was custom for each platform.

As I canвҖҷt find a image, hereвҖҷs the () one to show what it might look like.EDIT: So with the opening up a вҖңвҖң, Jason who is running that part, had a one and it turns out there isnвҖҷt an interface, just a user port connector. This makes sense as the already has everything it needs to read/write bytes in parallel.

This is the program, but the one was the same. It consisted of a built in text editor/assembler/debugger. The text editor could hold 8 files up to a maximum of each, though you could include as many files as you like. It assembled before your finger left the key, and squired down in under a second. This was a massive speed boost from the 20 minutes I sometimes spent waiting on the to disk assemble using a fast parallel disk interface.

The other good thing about , is it remembers the start date, and the last time you touched it вҖ“ which is very cool and handy. So I started this on 7th of August 1989вҖҰ. though, this probably isnвҖҷt right. This was the month the office opened, and I was still working on at this point. However, I did create a framework, and then probably based the game off that. The date below 2/10/89 looks better, and the end date looks about right 25/4/90. Games back then took about 6 months, so this also fits.

So in the main code of all my games, I setup equates (constants), and zero page and normal variables. On the there are bytes of fast zero page, are taken with control, and the rest are available for use. Zero page instructions typically take cycle less to operate, so are highly desirable for critical code optimisations.

These are some of the constants. At the top you can see the big ticket items вҖ“ memory map etc. ThereвҖҷs a couple of interesting items here вҖ“ /, and . The collision in wasnвҖҷt character based, it was bitmap based. I actually read the background tiles, and then masked whatever frame of the ship was active onto these characters. This was pretty slow, but allowed proper collision to the background вҖ“ something that was vital in later levels. IвҖҷll discuss this mode later of courseвҖҰ

вҖ“ this was where the debugger/downloader code lived. Whenever the hit a instruction, I would detect this in the it generated, and jumped to the debugger code.

This is how I allocated Zero Page variables. Prior to having good macros, IвҖҷd have to work these out manually, but now I was able to simple set a name and how many bytes I wanted, and the label would be set. I still use this method for variable allocation when need be вҖ“ itвҖҷs really useful.

I use the same method for sprite variable allocation. Have to sayвҖҰ this is a lot of variables for each sprite вҖ“ oh wellвҖҰ.

And this is where I defined general variables, and player structure. ThereвҖҷs of everything as is a simultaneous player game. This really effected the multiplexor вҖ“ but more on that laterвҖҰ.

Lastly for today, is the start up code, we take over everything and nuke stack and zero page, flick out etc and setup вҖ“ the usualвҖҰ

The in are complicated, and heavily tied to the multiplexor, so IвҖҷll talk about that next timeвҖҰ..

Also just to sayвҖҰ. as this was just my second game, and only a couple of months after the office opened, IвҖҷm amazed at the level of commenting in the source! рҹҳӮ
